data.title: Sendas Distribuidora: Deleveraging Delivered, Awaiting The Macro Turn
data.description:
In a previous work, my bullish call on Sendas Distribuidora (ASAIY) (but under the trade name Assaí) was based on three pillars.

The first was about a turning point in the Brazilian macro. Interest rates (Selic) are stuck at 15%, with the market beginning to price the end of the tightening cycle, with inflation converging within the margin limit stipulated by the Central Bank of Brazil—3% with ± 1.5% tolerance—and the historically strong negative correlation between ASAIY ADR and interest rates in the country.

The second pillar had to do with deleveraging as a catalyst. Assaí's net debt had fallen from ~4.7x at the peak of its leverage in 2023 to 3.17x in the last earnings print. The management team had set an explicit target of 2.6x by 4Q25, with 90% of EBITDA generated being directed to debt reduction, in addition to CapEx also being reduced with the maturation of the stores—officially welcoming the thesis for financial normalization and bidding farewell to the appeal of “aggressive growth”.

The third and final pillar was about ASAIY's valuation anchored in that same debt reduction. I argued back in September last year that there could be significant upside only via net debt reduction, as the multiple could expand with interest expenses—which comprised ~4% of total revenues—moderating. In other words, the broad scenario for Assaí was equity re-rate via capital structure improvement.

A couple of quarters have passed since then, so let's see where the thesis stands and whether these pillars have materialized to any extent.

Execution Intact Amid Deflationary Pressures

As the Brazilian cash and carry company reported its 4Q25 figures, I believe that the main point about Assaí's execution thesis was the achievement of the 2.6x leverage target. And yes, the company closed the quarter with 2.56x net debt/EBITDA (pre-IFRS).

Now, how does a company achieve this in a scenario where it has a nominal cost of debt of 16.3% per year? The answer would be the equation of: (1) strong cash generation; added to (2) capital discipline and lower CapEx needs; generating (3) a debt reduction faster than EBITDA fell.

Assaí generated R$2.8 billion in free cash flow in 2025, equivalent to roughly 84% EBITDA-to-cash conversion. This amount allowed the company to reduce net debt by R$1.2 billion during the year, even while net financial expenses increased to R$584 million in 4Q25 versus R$400 million in 4Q24.

Of course, financial expenses impact net income—not EBITDA. And as leverage is measured against EBITDA, the key driver was debt reduction funded by operational cash flow rather than lower interest costs.

As a result, a lower CapEx base of R$533 million, less than the same period last year, and reduced acquisition-related spending of a massive ~R$880 million helped accelerate deleveraging, while adjusted EBITDA reached R$3.7 billion (+16.8% QoQ; +3% YoY).

But going further, also very relevant in Assaí's latest print was the growth in revenues and same-store sales (volume) numbers.

Assaí posted consolidated net revenue of R$20.8 billion (+9.7% QoQ; +3.1% YoY). However, the headline 3% growth in top line does not tell the whole story. As the earnings call details, 4Q25 was marked by simultaneous deflation in multiple commodities relevant to the core of cash & carry—something somewhat unusual in historical terms. Staple items in the low-income Brazilian consumer basket saw significant price declines, such as rice, milk, and sugar plummeting by -37%, -16%, and -11%.

The key point here is that the volume sold in kilos and tons is growing. But what remained below inflation (measured by the IPCA, which came in at 4.4%) was nominal growth, precisely because Assaí is highly exposed to these categories of low-value addition and strong weight in the mix—i.e., Assaí sales rose, but earned less than inflation would suggest.

And there lies the explanation why same-store sales grew only 2.6% per year—even with traffic remaining stable at approximately 40 million customers per month. So the issue was much more about a mix pressured by deflation and trade-down than empty stores.

Another very relevant point for understanding Assaí's momentum (and the cash & carry scenario in Brazil) was the so-called "K-effect," introduced by the company's own management team. In this case, formats exposed to higher-income customers grew +4%, while formats more dependent on low income showed a decline of close to -9%. When we synchronize this with the macro backdrop in Brazil, it is easier to understand this bifurcation. According to data cited by Assaí's management team, Brazil has approximately R$7 trillion allocated to fixed income, while approximately R$4 trillion is in household debt. In other words, part of the population has benefited from high borrowing costs, while another part suffers from high interest rates and indebtedness.

In the case of Assaí, the side effect is a trade-down within customers' shopping carts (opting for cheaper brands), greater price sensitivity, and nominal pressure on same-store sales even with resilient volume. And for the B2B segment—which represents a considerable share of sales—there is still the component of greater sensitivity to the deflationary environment as small retailers reduce inventory replenishment to avoid losses from falling prices—which generates a cautious purchasing dynamic even without structural deterioration in final demand.

How Should Investors View ASAIY From Here?

Arguably, weaker nominal revenue growth does not invalidate Assai1's thesis, but rather qualifies it. If the pressure came mainly from price and mix rather than traffic or volume, then a normalization of commodities could re-accelerate nominal growth without the need for a demand "miracle."

Assaí recognizes that prices such as R$12 (~US$2) for rice are not structurally sustainable. If there is a partial recovery of these prices, the arithmetic effect on revenue tends to be immediate.

Therefore, the latest earnings print shows that Assaí relied on (1) resilient volume; (2) defensive traffic; (3) mix pressured by deflation; and (4) consumption bifurcation.

Within the broader context of the thesis—delivered deleveraging and maintained capital discipline—I see the revenue dynamics as more of a temporary headwind than a sign of structural deterioration.

All of this is important for how to look through the lens of valuation. I believe that starting in 4Q25, Assaí's valuation narrative may begin to detach itself from the defensive interest rate cut and move closer to a story of stock re-rating, for three reasons that are directly connected to operating metrics and the macro scenario itself.

In this case, if the main valuation driver of the last cycle was the disciplined execution of the deleveraging plan, based on the current EV/EBITDA of 6.4x, I would say that recent movements have already taken Assaí's ADR out of the "multiple distress" of 4.5x, but it is still far from returning to the average multiple of 8x. So here, I believe the interpretation is that deleveraging has been delivered, but the multiple has not yet normalized.

But I also would not expect the multiple to converge to 8x again with a macro scenario of high interest rates in Brazil and Assaí's nominal top line growth slowing down—even though deflation appears to be cyclical. As the Selic rate was kept at 15% at the end of January (even with inflation very close to the target), a cut seems inevitable at the next meetings of Brazil's monetary policy committee (Copom)—the next one being scheduled for March 18.

Giving a brief overview of why this is the scenario being priced in the Brazilian macro, the main reasons for believing in a cut in March can be explained by: (1) cumulative annual inflation hovering around ~4.3%–4.5% within the tolerance band, with agricultural commodities in deflation and services losing marginal traction—the ex-ante real interest rate is above 10%; (2) the output gap is widening with low-income consumption in Brazil under pressure (as Assaí itself showed in the call); and (3) the unemployment rate of ~7.5%-7.8% is still historically low but has stopped falling.

I believe there is a high probability that Copom will cut the Selic rate in March by 25 bps to 50 bps, which should ease Assaí's implied cost of capital and may support an expansion of multiples closer to the historical average (~8x)—although part of this assumption is already being priced in, but not entirely, in my view.

Risks and Final Remarks

The primary risk for ASAIY remains macroeconomic. If Brazil's central bank chooses to keep the Selic rate high for longer—whether due to fiscal deterioration, reanchoring of expectations, or exchange rate shocks—the cost of capital would remain under pressure and the normalization of multiples could take longer than the market anticipates. The persistence of the K-effect with structurally weak low income is also a considerable risk factor, despite indications that it is somewhat cyclical.

That said, I believe that 4Q25 marks an important turning point in Assaí's thesis, as deleveraging was successfully delivered, while cash generation proved robust under 16% nominal interest rates, and volume performed defensively despite price compression. With an imminent turnaround in the Brazilian macro environment, the narrative will tend to shift from "survival" equity to "normalization," and with that, I believe investors will be able to target multiples more in line with the median, which would be a relevant upside given the current asymmetry. Therefore, I reiterate the Buy rating on ASAIY.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Antero Midstream: Potential For $1.3 Billion In 2027 Adjusted EBITDA (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Antero Midstream (AM) ended 2025 with adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow after dividends that were around the high end of its revised guidance (which was an improvement over its original guidance). It expects its 2026 adjusted EBITDA growth to be driven by its HG Energy acquisition, but also suggests high-single-digit adjusted EBITDA growth in 2027. This would put its 2027 adjusted EBITDA around $1.3 billion, which I believe would support an increased $0.30 per share quarterly dividend (while keeping its free cash flow after dividends above $300 million).

I am increasing my estimate of Antero Midstream's value to $21.20 per share, based on that 2027 outlook and a 50/50 chance of winning $300+ million in the Veolia lawsuit. With Antero Midstream trading around that, I am moving back to a "Neutral" outlook on it.

Q4 2025 Results

Antero Midstream's recent results have been relatively consistent and at the higher end of expectations. Its low-pressure gathering and compression volumes were unchanged in Q4 2025 compared to Q3 2025, while its high-pressure gathering volumes were up 1% quarter-over-quarter.

This contributed to Antero Midstream generating $285 million in adjusted EBITDA in Q4 2025, which was a slight increase over its $281 million in adjusted EBITDA in Q3 2025 and its $284 million in adjusted EBITDA in Q2 2025.

This puts it at $1.125 billion in adjusted EBITDA for the full year, towards the high end of its revised guidance for $1.09 billion to $1.13 billion in adjusted EBITDA. Antero's 2025 guidance for adjusted EBITDA was increased by $10 million in Q2 2025.

Antero Midstream reported $86 million in free cash flow after dividends in Q4 2025. This allowed it to reduce its net debt to slightly below $3 billion at the end of 2025 as well as continue repurchasing shares. Antero Midstream repurchased 2.7 million shares for $48 million in Q4 2025, and had $336 million in remaining capacity under its share repurchase program at the end of 2025.

Antero Midstream ended up with $325 million in free cash flow after dividends for the full year, which was at the high end of its revised guidance for $275 million to $325 million in free cash flow after dividends. This revised guidance was also a $25 million increase over its original guidance for free cash flow after dividends.

2026 Guidance

Antero Midstream provided its 2026 guidance, which includes the impact of its HG Energy acquisition closing in early February and its Ohio Utica Shale divestiture occurring in late February.

I would say that its guidance looks to be fairly conservative. Antero Midstream expects $1.21 billion in 2026 adjusted EBITDA at its guidance midpoint, which is an 8% increase compared to 2025. That guidance would suggest roughly flat adjusted EBITDA growth from its legacy assets, though. If it can generate around 2% organic adjusted EBITDA growth instead, it would end up at the high end of its 2026 guidance.

In $ Millions 2026 Guidance
Adjusted EBITDA $1,210
Capital Expenditures $205
Interest $220
Cash Taxes $0
FCF Before Dividends $785
Dividends $425
FCF After Dividends $360

Antero Midstream also expects $360 million in free cash flow after dividends in 2026 at its guidance midpoint. This is an 11% increase compared to 2025. This number is incrementally increased by Antero Midstream's share repurchases reducing its dividend payments.

Potential 2027 Outlook

Antero Midstream expects that Antero Resources continuing to run three rigs and two completion crews will result in high-single digit adjusted EBITDA growth for Antero Midstream in 2027. Antero Midstream would also benefit slightly from a full year of contributions from the HG Energy assets.

Thus Antero Midstream could see around $1.3 billion in 2027 adjusted EBITDA. I can envision Antero Midstream increasing its dividend with that amount of projected adjusted EBITDA.

A quarterly dividend of $0.30 per share (close to the $0.3075 per quarter share dividend it had between mid-2019 to early 2021) would then result in Antero Midstream having around $320 million in free cash flow after dividends. This is similar to its 2025 free cash flow after dividends.

Notes On Valuation

I estimated Antero Midstream's value at approximately $20.20 per share after its HG Energy acquisition. I am increasing that estimate by $1 per share to $21.20 per share, which reflects Antero Midstream finishing near the high end of its updated guidance for 2025 and having potential to generate $1.3 billion in adjusted EBITDA in 2027.

This valuation estimate also assumes a 50/50 chance that it prevails in the Veolia lawsuit. If it wins $300+ million in the end, I would increase its estimated value to $21.50 per share.

Antero Midstream's current share price is above $21, so I'm moving back to a "Neutral" outlook on it.

Conclusion

Antero Midstream finished 2025 with adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow after dividends that were around the high end of its revised guidance. It also expects some improvements in 2026 that are being driven by its HG Energy acquisition (although I believe its 2026 guidance is conservative).

Antero Midstream expects solid adjusted EBITDA growth in 2027, which could push its adjusted EBITDA up to $1.3 billion (or more). This should be enough to support an increased quarterly dividend of $0.30 per share.

Antero Midstream's estimated value is now $21.20 per share, up $1 from my last estimate (after the HG Energy acquisition announcement). This is close to Antero Midstream's current share price after its significant gains in recent days.

data.title: AI Is Not My Biggest Concern With Stifel Financial (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
This article serves as an update to my previous analysis of Stifel Financial (SF) that was written in May of 2025. At that time, the company had just reported results from Q1 2025 that included a big shortfall on earnings due to elevated provisions for legal matters. Stifel had been ordered to pay $132.5 million after an arbitration panel determined the company misrepresented the risk involved for an investment of structured notes. My opinion of SF at that time was that the stock was worth buying in spite of this noise because of its strong position in the wealth management industry. Since my article was published, Stifel has gained 41.5%, over 19% more than the S&P 500.

As I write this follow-up today, things seem similar to where they were last May, but for different reasons. Over the last week, SF has taken a hit of over 8% for another concern, the creeping presence of artificial intelligence in the wealth management space. Privately held financial software company Altruist unveiled a new use for its AI platform known as “Hazel” that can create personalized tax strategies for clients “within minutes”. The high-net-worth financial services sector is just the latest to be impacted by concerns that AI will diminish future profit growth for traditional businesses.

I am not convinced that the sky is falling for SF and its peers, but I think the recent selloff for the stock may be justified for other reasons. The legal issues that were problematic for the company at the start of last year are not going away, and the valuation of its stock has become a little stretched. If the stock were to drop another 8 to 10% in the weeks ahead, I could be swayed to return to my Buy rating, but for now, I am updating my opinion of Stifel Financial to a Hold.

Company Overview

Stifel Financial Corporation defines itself as a diversified financial services holding company. It is perhaps best known for its brokerage and investment banking subsidiary, Stifel, Nicolaus & Company. SF has over half a trillion dollars in client assets under management, and the company has stated a public goal of reaching $1 trillion with no timeline set for that achievement. For a more detailed description of the company’s operations and history, readers are encouraged to look back at my article from last May.

SF currently has a market cap of $12.6 billion. In FY 2025, it collected over $5.5 billion in revenues, which was a record for the company. Global wealth management contributed 64.0% of that total. Stifel’s institutional operations, which include merger advisory and underwriting services, contributed the bulk of the remaining revenue at 34.6%. A more detailed report of the company’s financial results from Q4 2025 as well as the full year can be seen below.

SF currently has a forward yield of 1.63%. On January 27, the company announced that it was raising its quarterly dividend by 10.9% to $0.51 per share. Barring a decrease in the payout in the final three quarters of the year, 2025 will be the ninth straight year that the company has increased its total yearly distribution. While its overall yield is below the average for its sector, SF's five-year compound average dividend growth rate is over 32%.

Analysis of Stifel Financial

On January 28, Stifel Financial published its financial results from Q4 2025. Revenues in the quarter were $1.56 billion, an increase of 14.7% from the same quarter in 2024. Non-GAAP net income of $2.63 per share topped expectations by $0.12.

When all four quarters from 2025 are combined, Stifel put together a banner year, at least from a revenue perspective. The company produced all-time highs in net revenue, global wealth management revenue, and asset management revenue. SF also announced a three-for-two stock split that will become effective on February 27. The market tended to take most of this news in stride as SF fell by almost 0.1% on the day this news was released.

SF had a more attractive valuation when I first wrote about the company last May. At that time, SF traded with a trailing PE ratio of 14.8. Its PE for FY 2025 is now 18.6. The last time the stock traded at that high of a valuation was back in early 2018. Earnings growth for this year is projected to be just over 44%, however, making a higher multiple more reasonable. The forward PE for Stifel for FY 2026 is just 12.9.

When discussing the results in the Q4 2025 earnings release, Stifel Chairman and CEO Ronald Kruszewski said last year “marked a record year for Stifel and demonstrated the strength of [its] platform and long-term strategy.” He added, “While we remain attentive to market and geopolitical risks, we are confident in our ability to navigate uncertainty and continue to deliver for clients and shareholders.”

Yet, not everything from the last year was positive for the company from a trend perspective. SF's compensation expense ratio rose from 58.7% in FY 2024 to 59.2% last year. GAAP earnings for the full year actually declined when compared to the previous year by just over 6% to $5.87 per share. Return on common equity and return on tangible common equity, two non-GAAP measures reported by the company, dropped by 1.1% and 1.7%, respectively, due largely to elevated legal provisions.

Looking ahead to FY 2026, Stifel Financial expects revenues to rise 12%, from $5.5 billion to $6.2 billion. The compensation ratio, with non-GAAP calculations, is expected to drop 60 basis points. Non-GAAP earnings are expected to rise 22%. (Non-GAAP earnings do not take out provisions for legal matters.)

Risks to Consider

The legal issues that were publicized at the start of 2025 have not gone away. A recent appeal of the $132.5 million lawsuit was denied. Stifel has until February 20 to object to the recommendations from the most recent court decision. Just one month ago, Stifel paid out an $850,000 settlement to settle a related case. The company faces a total of 23 pending cases, all related to the same former employee. Stifel’s current running total of arbitration awards and settlements is now almost $182 million. There is an additional lawsuit pending over excessive fees and poor services for 401(k) advisement that was filed last July. I can find no reputable current estimate of what that case could cost the company. I expect these matters to be a cloud that hovers over the company for at least the first half of this year.

Stifel’s investment newsletter that was published last month, outlined several potential threats to the overall economy both here in the US and abroad. Higher levels of market expansion and economic activity are a needed tailwind for Stifel’s business operations. Included in this newsletter were the following threats for 2026:

  • Instability caused by the midterm elections in the US
  • Rising debt levels in the US that reduce confidence in US treasuries and the US dollar
  • Inflation staying well above the Fed’s 2% target
  • Unknown impact from changes in leadership at the Federal Reserve
  • Disruption caused by artificial intelligence

Any number of these concerns could make it challenging for Stifel to meet its projections for top and bottom-line growth in the year ahead as trading volumes and fee generation is suppressed. The loan portfolio controlled by Stifel's banking operations could face higher default rates with a significant economic slowdown.

Yes, artificial intelligence is a potential threat to the future for Stifel Financial and its peers, but I am not as concerned about this at the current time. There is no reason that SF cannot use its own AI tools to assist clients in the future. As AI improves efficiencies in other areas, Stifel expects it will be an amplifier, not a detractor, for its earnings. The company may have to increase some expenses to stay current with the latest innovations in its sector, but I list this risk last on my list for a reason, as I see it as the smallest from the ones listed here. So much of what the company does on a daily basis goes beyond creating tax strategies. At least for now, Stifel's business is still very much relationship driven, especially for activities like research and M&A. These activities require trust and judgment that cannot be fully replicated by AI, at least, not yet.

The Bottom Line

When everything is considered together, I do think that Stifel Financial could be set up for another solid performance in 2026. However, when I rated the stock as a Buy previously, I was working with the belief that it would outperform the market. Now, I believe its performance will be more in line with its peers. This year could be a strong year for the financial sector overall, so exposure to the investment banking and brokerage sector may pay off. However, investors can buy stock in a peer company like Moelis and Company (MC) to get the same exposure without the risk of the lingering legal matters.

Stifel Financial is considered a Hold by Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating scale. The company’s lowest score is in valuation, stemming from the company’s high PE ratio for the last year when compared to peers from its sector. Stifel’s Profitability score is also impacted by the company’s margins, which are lower than the sector median as well as its own five-year average for that metric.

In downgrading SF to a Hold, I am not suggesting that the wheels are completely falling off for the company's operations. I just do not think the stock represents the same potential bargain that it did last May.

data.title: FCT: No Near-Term Growth Catalyst
data.description:
Overview

With market indices pulling back from their all-time highs, some investors may be looking for a safe haven. First Trust Senior Floating Rate Income Fund II (FCT) provides exposure to a diverse range of income-producing debt securities, which can offer stability that is disconnected from traditional equities. When I previously covered FCT, I issued a hold rating due to the limited growth potential due to high interest rates at the time. Since my last coverage, the interest rate environment has shifted, and the fund has released an updated semi-annual report for 2026, which prompted me to revisit the fund's value proposition now that we've crossed into the new year.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that FCT's share price has declined by about 4.6%. The fund has failed to participate in the market rally over the last few months but still remains resilient during this period of elevated interest rates. When including all distributions that were paid to shareholders, the total return jumps up to nearly 7.2% over the same time frame. FCT now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 11.7% while issuing payouts on a monthly basis. However, I remain cautious about the overall sustainability of the current payouts if performance does not improve.

Despite the fund's suppressed share price over the last few years, FCT still trades at the higher end of its price to NAV range. Therefore, valuation can be a bit tricky here, but I don't believe that this is the time to accumulate yet. Additionally, the fund's NAV growth has been limited since its earnings haven't substantially improved yet. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that FCT implements to generate its earnings for shareholders.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, FCT has total managed assets of $292.7M that are spread across a diverse range of income-producing securities. The fund has a listed expense ratio of 2.42%, but this is a bit misleading since it includes the fund's costs related to interest expenses and other operating fees. Since FCT is structured as a closed-end fund, it is legally required to bulk in these costs as part of its expense ratio. When excluding these other costs, the actual management fee sits at 0.92% of net assets, which is the actual cost that investors see.

The fund has a primary goal of generating a high current income so that it can produce a high rate of distributions. In order to achieve this goal, FCT aims to allocate at least 80% of its assets to senior loans. This is ideal because senior secured loans sit at the top of the corporate capital structure and have the highest priority for repayment, which protects the fund from losing all invested capital in a bad investment. The breakdown by investment type is as follows:

  • Senior Floating-Rate Loan Interests: ~96.72%
  • Corporate Bonds and Notes: ~2.13%
  • Foreign Corporate Bonds and Notes: ~1.01%

Expanding on this, FCT also aims to maintain an efficient level of diversity across industries to help mitigate any sort of concentration risks. As we can see below, FCT is most heavily allocated to insurance securities at 16.39% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to software and health care providers, which amounts to 15.02% and 7.94% of assets, respectively.

I like the heavy allocation to insurance-based securities because these are businesses that typically have consistent cash flows and low turnover. So this is an industry that may be solid for debt securities since they can offer stability during uncertain market environments. Similarly, the allocation to software securities can also be great because these tend to be subscription-based businesses that offer stable cash flows and may be more resilient to shifts in the technology market.

Lastly, the fund provides a ton of exposure to private entities. This expands the level of diversity within the fund and can be a solid introduction for investors that want to obtain a hedge against the public equities market. However, we should remember that FCT maintains exposure to these positions through debt securities, so the fund may not be able to directly participate in the growth of its own holdings.

Valuation & Performance Insights

At the time of my last coverage, FCT traded at a discount to NAV of 3.74%. Despite the share price declining in recent months, FCT now trades at a slightly smaller discount to NAV of 1.20%. For reference, the fund has traded at an average discount to NAV of 5.89% over the last five years. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that FCT now trades at the higher end of its historical price-to-NAV range over the last ten years. Therefore, I believe that it is still not a good time to accumulate shares.

Since my last coverage, FCT released an updated semi-annual report for 2026. This is the most updated report that provides transparency into the fund's earnings. Net investment income amounted to $0.36 per share, while net realized gains came in flat. Looking back at prior years, FCT is aligned to underperform its typical range for net investment income, assuming a similar performance over the remainder of the period. This could be an indication that the portfolio experienced some unfavorable shifts or the direct impact from lower interest rates.

Since the time of my last coverage, the Fed initiated three separate 25 basis point cuts to interest rates throughout 2025. Since the portfolio is primarily comprised of floating rate debt securities, a lower interest rate can have a direct impact on the net investment income that the fund captures. Assuming that interest rates continue to trend lower over the next twelve months, we may see net investment income also trend lower. Unless management is capable of allocating capital towards new investment activity to offset these losses, I believe there's a good chance that we'll see earnings trend lower in the near term.

One of the issues is that net investment income doesn't sufficiently cover the distributions that are being paid. Therefore, FCT is reliant on positive net realized gains in order to bridge that gap in its earnings. The issue is that net realized gains can wildly vary based on market conditions, and this makes it hard to accurately predict how the underlying NAV will perform over time. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts over the last few years:

  • 2021: $12.70 per share
  • 2022: $11.30 per share
  • 2023: $10.96 per share
  • 2024: $11.03 per share
  • 2025: $10.58 per share
  • 2026 (semi-annual): $10.36 per share

Since the fund is dependent on net realized gains, FCT may find itself in a position where it is paying out more than it actually earns sometimes. This is most likely to occur during periods of market declines, which is when FCT may see net realized losses. So while market indices have continued to trade at all-time highs, FCT is not structurally capable of participating in this growth. As we can see below, FCT's share price has declined by 16.8% over the last five years, which severely underperforms against the S&P 500's (SPY) price growth of 73.5% over the same time frame. Even when including all distributions that were paid out, FCT still severely underperforms a more standard index-tracking ETF.

Dividend Risk

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.097 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 11.7%. If we take this monthly distribution and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of $1.164 per share. Referring to the same semi-annual report, FCT's actual dividend coverage remains very inconsistent. Since 2021, FCT has only been able to generate sufficient earnings in two of the last five years. If interest rates continue to trend downward and FCT's earnings worsen, I think there is a solid chance that distributions will be cut over the next twelve months.

For this reason, it is hard to recommend a position in FCT for income investors at this time. The whole reason that investors maintain exposure to a high-yielding fund like this is for the income that it can provide. If that income is under the threat of reduction, it defeats the whole purpose of maintaining a position. This is just the nature of floating rate funds, so FCT may not be the best buy-and-hold position for income investors seeking stability.

Lastly, investors should consider the risk that the distributions received from FCT do not offer much tax-efficiency. For instance, the latest section19(a) notice indicates that the majority of the payouts issued YTD were classified as net investment income, which has the greatest tax burden for investors. Therefore, a position in FCT would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors that want to limit their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that FCT remains a hold at this time. In an environment where interest rates continue to trend lower, I don't believe there are any near-term catalysts that can help the fund's underlying earnings improve. A lower interest rate environment will translate to lower net investment income. Additionally, I believe that the lower earnings will threaten the current distributions. Therefore, I anticipate that payouts may be cut in the near-term if earnings are not improved. Lastly, the fund trades at one of the least attractive price-to-NAV valuations over the last decade, which further reinforces that this is not an ideal time for accumulation.

data.title: Upstart: A Directional Bet On Falling Rates
data.description:
Upstart (UPST) beat Wall Street estimates for the fourth fiscal quarter on Tuesday amid a strong product uptake in a favorable lending environment. Upstart is going to benefit from lower federal fund rates in 2026, and with major platform metrics like originations surging, especially in personal loans, the Fintech is set for significant growth.

Shares of Upstart crashed 15% on Wednesday, however, as the Fintech informed investors about a leadership transition, which added uncertainty, and the firm's lack of specific quarterly profitability guidance disappointed as well. In my opinion, investors are overreacting to the Q4 report: Upstart's earnings showed healthy origination metrics and strong growth in transaction volumes, so I believe that investors may want to see the correction as an engagement opportunity. The Fintech's shares are no longer highly valued -- with a forward P/E ratio of 10.6X -- and I see considerable rebound potential in 2026 if my thesis plays out as expected.

Previous rating

I rated shares of Upstart a strong buy in my last work on the AI-supported credit platform -- Strong Platform Growth, High Margins, Cheap P/E -- due to favorable automation trends and improving channel conversions that allowed for a higher number of loans to get approved.

Upstart maintained strong growth momentum in the personal loan category in the last quarter, and ended FY 2025 with record originations in the segment. With interest rates set to contract in 2026 and AI adoption growing, I am looking forward to seeing more partnerships with financial institutions and credit unions, and would not be surprised to see an acceleration of top-line and origination growth this year.

Upstart beat Q4’25 estimates

The AI lending start-up beat top and bottom line estimates by moderate margins on Tuesday, mainly due to strong origination momentum in the dominant personal loan category: Upstart published $0.50 per share in adjusted earnings for Q4'25 which outmatched the consensus estimate by $0.03 per share. Revenues also beat, by $7.5M, and were posted at $296.1M.

Upstart has been a top growth pick of mine in the Fintech category in the last year because the platform was uniquely positioned to benefit from growing transaction volumes in a falling-rate world. Higher origination and transaction volumes on the Upstart platform are the results of two main developments: 1) Upstart’s growing platform adoption is leading to wider reach, and 2) Lower base interest rates stimulate credit demand.

This backdrop has led to a sustained upswing in transaction volumes throughout 2025, and the backdrop for 2026 is at least as enticing: Upstart published a total transaction volume of $3.2B for the fourth fiscal quarter -- an all-time quarterly record -- which showed a year-over-year growth rate of 52%. Transaction volumes grow as more loans get approved utilizing Upstart's technology stack, which in turn is responsible for a massive increase in the firm's top line.

The main driver of this growth is personal loans, which really are Upstart’s bread and butter and which is why the Fintech has been a darling of Wall Street in recent years. Personal loan originations surged to a record $2.9B in Q4'25, showing 41% year-over-year growth. However, Upstart has ventured into other loans as well recently, such as auto loans and Home Equity Lines of Credit (HELOCs) which carry thinner margins than the very profitable personal loan segment. As the Fintech expands its product portfolio and includes lower-margin loans, a shifting revenue mix could present a challenge for Upstart's margin development.

Key catalyst for revenue growth is intact

Since the Federal Reserve lowered interest rates by 25 basis points in December and inflation is broadly moderating, the Fintech is well-positioned to see strong demand for its credit solutions throughout 2026. I expect the Federal Reserve to lower interest rates more than the two times that the market expects right now (I expect at least three cuts of 25 basis points each this year). The reason for my more bullish stance is that inflation is no longer a core challenge to consumers and businesses, and with inflation falling below long term rates, the Federal Reserve actually has a strong incentive to be much more aggressive with rate cuts in 2026.

Interest rate cuts would obviously benefit the firm’s revenue trajectory as well. In the fourth quarter, Upstart generated $296M in total revenue, showing 35% year-over-year growth. While the Fintech’s results showed a revenue deceleration compared to the previous quarter (+71% year-over-year growth in Q3’25), new rate cuts could lead to an acceleration of revenues in 2026, especially if the company proves to be adept at growing other loan categories, like HELOCs.

While Upstart's margins may compress going forward due to the addition of lower-margin loan categories, the Fintech has an opportunity to diversify its business here and overall growth transaction volumes. This should lead to revenue tailwinds and positive earnings growth... which could result in a higher valuation multiplier for Upstart.

Outlook, CEO change, lack of profitability guidance

As far as the outlook for FY 2026 is concerned, Upstart expects to generate $1.4B in total revenue, which implies a year-over-year growth rate of 35%. The Fintech also announced that it would stop providing quarterly financial guidance, which added uncertainty on Wednesday. In addition, the Fintech's CEO Dave Girouard is going to step down from his role on May 1, 2026 and will be replaced by the firm's CTO Paul Gu.

Leadership transitions often create investor uncertainty, although I believe that in Upstart's case investors have no reason to be anxious: Paul Gu is also a co-founder of the Fintech and has a 14-year track record in building the start-up into the current Upstart-branded lending platform. I believe he is well-suited to lead Upstart's next phase of growth. What may have weighed more heavily on Upstart's share price following Tuesday's earnings report is the lack of future quarterly earnings guidance.

As a result of this leadership transition and Upstart's announcement to cease giving full-year profit guidance, the Fintech lost ~$800M in market cap. Investors should note that Upstart did not downgrade its profit guidance, it merely said that it would no longer give a full-year earnings outlook. This, in my opinion, is the reason why I feel that investors overreacted.

Upstart’s valuation

Upstart is currently trading at a forward (FY 2027) price-to-earnings ratio of 10.6X, which makes the company a very low-priced Fintech investment, in both a historical and comparative context. Since Upstart is uniquely positioned to benefit from lower federal fund rates -- which should drastically boost the platform’s origination and transaction volumes this year -- I don't see an impaired investment case at all.

Upstart's less than 11.0X forward P/E compares to a P/E ratio of 15.4X for Affirm (AFRM) and a 26.9X P/E ratio for SoFi Technologies (SOFI). SoFi just reported exceptionally strong customer growth in the fourth-quarter and submitted an impressive growth outlook for 2026 as well, which I discussed in my work here: Strong Revaluation Catalyst.

In my last coverage on Upstart, I calculated a fair value of the Fintech in the neighborhood of $58 per-share, which was based off of a fair value P/E of ~23.0X... which was the industry group average at the time. Upstart’s surging transaction volumes and revenue justify a higher price-to-earnings ratio than 10.6X, in my opinion, as does the fact that the Fintech should be a clear winner in a rate-cutting market. The valuation multipliers mentioned here are based off of non-GAAP earnings estimates for the next full fiscal year, provided by Seeking Alpha.

Further, with Upstart's valuation dropping lately -- in large part due to profit taking -- I believe the risk profile is highly skewed to the upside here. If shares of Upstart were to revalue only to the Fintech industry group average of 17.6X, which is entirely possible in a lower-rate world benefiting loan originations, Upstart could have a fair value of ~$55/share (+66% upside revaluation potential).

Risks with Upstart

I see three key risks for Upstart that relate to a variety of factors: First, a delay in federal fund rate cuts would likely be a negative catalyst for my expectation for accelerating origination growth in the personal loan category. Second, deteriorating platform growth would most likely pose a serious valuation risk going forward. Lastly, Upstart has had success in driving AI product adoption, but the platform still relies mainly on personal loans. A shift towards more originations in other loan categories (which have lower margins) may slow the firm's earnings and margin growth in the future.

Final thoughts

The 15% drop in price on Wednesday is totally exaggerated and cannot be justified by Upstart's strong fourth-quarter earnings scorecard... which was impressive not only in the sense that it beat Wall Street estimates, but also in terms of origination growth. Further, the CEO transition is not really a good reason to sell shares, especially not with Upstart presenting strong guidance for FY 2026 as well.

I like that Upstart is growing extremely quickly and has a real shot at 30%+ growth in 2026, which could be catalyzed by falling federal fund rates. In my opinion, the market reacted too negatively to the Fintech’s earnings report and guidance... and with shares trading at a low 10.6X forward earnings, I would actually make the argument that Upstart is egregiously mispriced now.

data.title: AIER's Everyday Price Index Edges Up 0.33 Percent As Inflation Shifts To Services
data.description:
By Peter C. Earle

AIER’s own index posted its largest gain since June, but gains were largely cancelled out by moderation in other categories. Inflation pressures seem to be shifting rather than surging, with gradual disinflation still the dominant trend.

In January 2026, the AIER Everyday Price Index (EPI) rose 0.33 percent to 298.0, starting the year with its largest increase since June 2025. Seventeen of its 24 constituents rose in price in January, with five declining and two unchanged.

Pets and pet products, gardening and lawncare services, and housing fuels and utilities saw the largest monthly price increases, while alcoholic beverages at home, personal care products, and intercity transportation saw the largest declines.

AIER Everyday Price Index vs. US Consumer Price Index (NSA, 1987 = 100)

Also on February 13, 2026, the US Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) released its January 2026 Consumer Price Index (CPI) data. On the month-over-month side, headline CPI rose 0.2 percent (versus a surveyed 0.3 percent) while core increased the forecast 0.3 percent.

January 2026 US CPI headline and core month-over-month (2016–present)

Consumer prices in January were driven largely by a moderate rise in core inflation, with the index excluding food and energy increasing 0.3 percent for the month. Price gains were broad-based across services and discretionary categories, including sharp increases in airline fares alongside advances in personal care, recreation, medical care, communication, apparel, and new vehicles.

These increases were partly offset by declines in used cars and trucks, household furnishings and operations, and motor vehicle insurance, reflecting ongoing normalization in some durable goods and insurance-related costs. Within medical care, hospital services and physicians’ services moved higher, while prescription drug prices were unchanged, contributing to steady upward pressure in healthcare costs.

Food prices rose 0.2 percent in January, led by gains across most grocery categories, including cereals and bakery products, dairy, meats, nonalcoholic beverages, and fruits and vegetables, while the “other food at home” category declined modestly.

Prices for meals away from home edged up 0.1 percent, with increases in limited-service meals offset by flat pricing at full-service establishments. Energy prices, by contrast, fell 1.5 percent over the month, driven primarily by a 3.2 percent decline in gasoline prices and a slight drop in electricity, although natural gas prices increased.

Overall, the mix of softer energy costs and firmer core categories left headline inflation shaped by continued resilience in services and selective goods inflation even as energy provided a temporary offset.

Over the prior 12 months, the headline Consumer Price Indices rose 2.4 percent against an expected 2.5 percent, with core year-over-year rising an expected 2.5 percent from January 2025 to January 2026.

January 2026 US CPI headline and core year-over-year (2016–present)

Over the 12 months ending in January, food prices continued to firm, with grocery costs rising 2.1 percent on the year as most major categories posted gains. Prices for nonalcoholic beverages led the increase, climbing 4.5 percent, while cereals and bakery products advanced 3.1 percent and meats, poultry, fish, and eggs rose 2.2 percent.

The “other food at home” category also increased 2.1 percent, and fruits and vegetables posted a more modest 0.8 percent gain, partially offset by a slight 0.3 percent decline in dairy and related products.

Dining out remained a notable source of inflation, with the food away from home index rising 4.0 percent over the year, driven by a 4.7 percent increase in full-service meals and a 3.2 percent rise in limited-service meals.

Energy prices were broadly flat over the year, edging down 0.1 percent overall as a sharp 7.5 percent decline in gasoline prices was largely counterbalanced by sizable increases in electricity and natural gas, which rose 6.3 percent and 9.8 percent respectively.

Excluding food and energy, core consumer prices increased 2.5 percent over the past year, with shelter costs advancing 3.0 percent and continuing to anchor underlying inflation.

Additional upward pressure came from medical care, household furnishings and operations, recreation, and personal care - the latter posting a notable 5.4 percent gain - even as some goods categories such as used vehicles and certain household items showed signs of cooling.

The January report came in milder than many economists expected - especially for a month that typically runs hot as businesses reset prices at the start of the year. Yet beneath the surface, the inflation story remains uneven.

Core goods prices were flat overall, masking a split between rising recreation-related items - such as consumer electronics, sporting goods, and toys - and declines in used vehicles, medical commodities, and some household goods.

These crosscurrents reflect several forces at work simultaneously: lingering tariff pass-through in certain goods, AI-driven demand for electronic inputs, regulatory changes holding down medical costs, and fading supply disruptions in groceries.

Services inflation, however, continues to run warmer, led by airfares, car rentals, and admission prices for sporting events. Shelter inflation moderated, with both rents and owner-equivalent rents slowing, offering a potential sign that one of the largest drivers of recent inflation is gradually cooling. Notably, prescription drug prices were unchanged - unusual for January - partly due to negotiated Medicare pricing that offset typical annual increases.

Taken together, the report suggests inflation pressures are shifting rather than disappearing. Discretionary services tied to travel, recreation, and wealth-effect spending remain firm, even as goods prices soften and everyday essentials such as energy and groceries show signs of relief.

Price increases also became more widespread across categories - a common January phenomenon - but the overall pace was far more restrained than in recent years, hinting that underlying disinflation may dominate in coming months if current trends hold.

Financial markets interpreted the data as supportive of potential Federal Reserve rate cuts later this year, though bond-market reactions were mixed given persistent strength in services inflation.

For households, the takeaway is that while inflation hasn’t vanished, the early-2026 trend looks less like a renewed surge and more like a gradual cooling - with pockets of stubborn price growth that policymakers will continue to watch closely.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Consumer Price Index: Inflation At 2.4% In January, Lower Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Inflation cooled for a third straight month in January as the headline figure for the Consumer Price Index came in at 2.4% year-over-year. This is down from 2.7% in December and was lower than the forecast of 2.5%. Additionally, the core CPI was at 2.5% year-over-year, down from 2.6% in December and in line with the forecast.

On a monthly basis, consumer prices were up 0.2% versus the projected 0.3% monthly growth, while core prices were up 0.3%, as expected. Here is the introduction from the BLS summary, which leads with the seasonally adjusted monthly data:

The Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers (CPI-U) increased 0.2 percent on a seasonally adjusted basis in January, the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics reported today. Over the last 12 months, the all items index increased 2.4 percent before seasonal adjustment.

The index for shelter rose 0.2 percent in January and was the largest factor in the all items monthly increase. The food index increased 0.2 percent over the month as did the food at home index, while the food away from home index rose 0.1 percent. These increases were partially offset by the index for energy, which fell 1.5 percent in January.

The index for all items less food and energy rose 0.3 percent in January. Indexes that increased over the month include airline fares, personal care, recreation, medical care, and communication. The indexes for used cars and trucks, household furnishings and operations, and motor vehicle insurance were among the major indexes that decreased in January.

The all items index rose 2.4 percent for the 12 months ending January, after rising 2.7 percent for the 12 months ending December. The all items less food and energy index rose 2.5 percent over the last 12 months. The energy index decreased 0.1 percent for the 12 months ending January. The food index increased 2.9 percent over the last year.

The first chart is an overlay of headline CPI and core CPI (excludes food and energy) since the turn of the century. The highlighted two percent level is the Federal Reserve's target inflation rate.

In January, price pressures eased from 2.68% in December to 2.39%, the lowest level since May. Meanwhile, core prices cooled from 2.64% in December to 2.50% in January, the lowest level since March 2021.

The next chart shows both series since 1957, the year the government first began tracking core inflation. In recent years, we have seen some of the highest inflation rates since the second of the two recessions in the early 1980s.

Consumer Price Index Components

The Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) divides all expenditures into eight categories and assigns a relative size to each, which is shown in the chart below. The BLS weighs these annually, with the latest weighting taking place in December 2025.

Here is a table showing the annualized change in headline and core CPI, not seasonally adjusted, for each of the past six months. Also included are the eight components of Headline CPI and a separate entry for energy, which is a collection of sub-indexes in housing and transportation.

We can make some inferences about how inflation is impacting our personal expenses depending on our relative exposure to the individual components. Some of us have higher transportation costs, others medical costs, etc. A conspicuous feature in the year-over-year table is the volatility in energy, significantly a result of gasoline prices, which is also reflected in transportation.

Here is the same table as above with month-over-month numbers (not seasonally adjusted).

Inflation: Fed's 2% Target

The Fed is on record as having a target rate of 2% for core inflation. The Fed traditionally uses the PCE Price Index as their preferred inflation gauge; however, the Consumer Price Index is another common measure.

The COVID-19 pandemic helped launch inflation into its highest levels since the 1980s. As a result, the Fed has been battling high inflation over the past few years with its monetary policy. Inflation has eased off of its 2022 highs; however, the back half of 2024 and most of 2025 have shown just how sticky it can be.

The Fed concluded its first meeting of 2026 by holding the federal funds rate (FFR) steady in the 3.50%-3.75% range. This move came after three consecutive rate cuts of 25 basis points each and keeps the central bank's range at its lowest level since November 2022.

The statement from the meeting revealed the Committee believes "inflation remains somewhat elevated," but that they are strongly committed to returning inflation to its 2% objective.

The Fed will meet again in mid-March, where they are expected to hold rates steady. At the time of writing, the CME FedWatch Tool shows a 92% likelihood the Fed will hold rates where they are versus an 8% likelihood of a 25 basis point reduction.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Apollo Global: Overdone Credit Fears Create A Buying Opportunity (Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Apollo Global Management, Inc. (APO) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing about 20% of their value. While the company continues to report excellent fundraising results, concerns about private equity and credit, with new concerns about the software sector, have significantly dampened investor sentiment. This has come even as most of its revenue is recurring in nature.

Even a solid Q4 has done little to boost shares. I last covered shares in January, and while I was right to downgrade Apollo, I should have moved all the way to a “Sell” given the 16% drop. That raises the question of whether now is a good time to buy into this beaten-up but high-quality company.

In the company’s fourth quarter, Apollo Global Management earned $2.47 per share, which blew past estimates by $0.43. We saw continued strength in fund flows, and investment performance was resilient. As such, I view APO as an increasingly compelling "Buy" opportunity.

AUM Growth Drives Fees Higher

Within its asset management segment, fee-related earnings (“FRE”) reached a record $690 million in Q4, and at $2.5 billion, they were up 23% from last year. Management fees jumped $200 million to $742 million, and compensation was up a relatively modest $109 million, as the business continues to enjoy meaningful operating leverage. Credit fees jumped 22% to $669 million, while equity fees were up 42% to $273 million.

Assets under management rose 25% to $938 billion. Beyond market appreciation, strong inflows continue to drive growth. In Q4 alone, it raised $42 billion. Fee-generating AUM was $709 billion. While widely thought of as a private equity company, Apollo is primarily a credit investor, as you can see below. The ongoing growth in fee-generating is particularly important. In general, Apollo can only begin charging fees as it deploys capital.

During the quarter, it deployed $113 billion, and this strong pace of origination should help it draw on uncalled capital and convert it into fee-generating assets. APO ended the year with $73 billion of dry powder, down about 2% sequentially. Still, this backlog provides essentially 10% of potential growth relative to its existing asset base, creating clear visibility of future revenue growth. Of course, as evidenced by $42 billion of flows this past quarter, this backlog is not the extent of future growth; the total should be much higher. But even without any new net flows, there is a runway for growth. Additionally, with the majority of AUM being perpetual in nature, its business is much more resilient against outflows than traditional asset managers who offer daily liquidity. With its backlog and ongoing flows, there is room for double-digit growth that extends several years.

Credit Fears Are Overdone At Athene

Turning to Retirement Services, spread-related earnings rose to $865 million, up about 2.5% from a year ago. Of course, Apollo also owns an insurer, Athene, and net invested assets were up 18% to $292 billion. Its base investment spread contracted 1 bp sequentially to 120 bps as higher-margin legacy policies rolled down. Spreads have also been weighed down by below-target alternative investments, as elevated rates have weakened private equity and real estate returns industrywide. Returns were $28 million below target at $344 million, a 4bp headwind. I expect an equally sluggish return environment this year. Apollo has aggressively grown this business over years.

Now, private credit has been a source of increased focus. Importantly, 97% of Athene’s assets are investment grade. Plus, as you can see below, performance continued to be strong in the fourth quarter, and Apollo is a seasoned underwriter. I was also encouraged by data on the diversification of its portfolio.

On the earnings call, management said its software exposure in private equity and on Athene’s balance is sub-1%. Its direct lending private credit business has mid-teens exposure to the sector. As such, total software exposure is about 2% of AUM. This is substantially less than peers, and APO estimates over 30% of private credit origination over the past decade has been to the sector. It is also important to remember that most of its investments are first-lien. Even if equity values decline, that does not mean companies cannot repay their debt, and Apollo’s funds are first in line to get paid back.

There have undoubtedly been some excesses in the private credit market. We also seem to be in a market environment where investors sell first and ask questions later the moment “AI disruption” becomes a focus point. While much remains to be seen about the ultimate impact of the AI, such fear-driven moments often lead to exaggerated moves. In general, I view problems in the sector as unlikely to be systemic. The biggest risk to Apollo would be if we saw sustained outflows from the asset class, thereby reducing fee revenue, but this seems unlikely, especially given the long-dated nature of its AUM.

Shares Are Now A "Buy"

Shares offer a 1.7% dividend yield, and management plans a 10% dividend increase this year. During the fourth quarter, it did $260 million of buybacks. However, this really just offsets equity issuance to employees. Apollo maintains an excellent balance sheet. It has $3.3 billion of cash, $1.8 billion of accrued performance fees, and $3.2 billion of investments, against $5.5 billion of debt. This translates to about $5/share.

The company is targeting at least 20% FRE growth in 2026. The majority of growth comes from its existing business, but we should also start to see benefits from new ventures. It has launched an ETF (PRIV), and eased regulations on 401(K) plans could open them to alternative investments. I expect a fairly slow ramp here even as the long-term opportunity is material. Given current AUM levels and its backlog, I view 20+% growth as very achievable.

Back in January, I saw fair value in the $150 zone given $4 of fee-related earnings, $7 of net cash and investments, and a 1.5x book value multiple on Athene. I still view $4 of FRE as likely, and with its net investment position, I see this being worth ~$75, valuing these recurring fees at 17.5x. Its asset management business does not have that much direct exposure to private credit losses, since it just earns a management fee, irrespective of performance. Its impact is more second derivative in nature. Plus, with its lower software exposure and solid returns, APO may actually gain market share from other managers.

Conversely, a significant wave of defaults could reduce spread income in Athene. Its Retirement Services business has a tangible book value of about $29 billion, or about $47/share. With shares at $125, investors are implicitly valuing Athene at just over 1x book ($75 from its Asset Management business and $47 Athene book value would be $122 for APO).

In this environment, a 1.5x book value multiple for Athene may be too aggressive, but 1x book value is quite attractive given its low-risk liabilities and high-quality investment portfolio. Using 1.5x book but assuming another 100 bps of credit losses (~$4 billion), which implies a significant default wave, fair value would be $139. At $125, shares have 11% upside to this curtailed price target that reflects incremental private credit risk. As such, I think shares have become too beaten up, and this pullback represents an opportunity. I am upgrading APO to “Buy” and recommend adding on this weakness.

data.title: GigaCloud: One Of The Main Diversification Bets From AI (Preview)
data.description:
Introduction

I wanted to revisit one of my main holdings, GigaCloud Technology Inc. (GCT), before it reports its Q4 earnings on the 2nd of March to see what needs to happen and what I believe will happen, which prompted me to add to my position as one of the companies that helps me achieve my diversification efforts away from AI.

What To Expect

We can see that analysts are expecting a sequential revenue decline in the upcoming quarter, or around 2%. However, the $327m revenue expectations translate to around 10% y/y growth. This is in line with the previous quarter’s results, as expected. Management’s expectations for the upcoming quarter are between $328m and $344m, which is $336m at the midpoint, leading to around 12% higher sales compared to last year’s Q4. The management didn’t provide us any guidance for profitability, but there are reasons to believe that we will see decent performance there.

Last quarter, analysts seemed to be caught off guard as the company beat expectations massively. So, analysts’ expectations of $0.85 and $0.58 adjusted and GAAP EPS, respectively, may be too low. Over the last ten quarters, we can see that analysts have been consistently underestimating the earnings power of GCT, as well as sales, which could mean we will see the results surprise to the upside once again.

Overall, it looks like analysts are not as bullish on the company’s Q4 performance. The lower sales expectations and quite low EPS numbers point to the company beating comfortably and showing some decent share price appreciation on the day. That is, if everything goes as well as I hope. Famous last words? We will see if my predictions age like wine or milk. We can also see that the hypergrowth stage of sales has ended, and from here on out, we should expect much more sustainable growth going forward. It is hard to tell where it is going to settle down, but low double digits is a possibility.

What I'm Looking For

I think for me, the most important item on the agenda will be regarding the company’s profitability going forward. The company’s margins have been on a downtrend over the last two years or so, which could put quite a few investors in a state of worry. I’m concerned about the way it has been going, and there could be further downside on the horizon in terms of profitability due to the persistent tariff worries, macroeconomic uncertainties, and sacrificing this profitability to gain a higher market share in the long run. However, there are reasons to believe that this is not going to last too much longer. I believe that the company’s exposure to tariffs is going to rapidly come down over the next year or two because of the management’s initiative of expanding outside the U.S., particularly in Europe. We saw how explosive the growth continues to be in Germany, with around 69% growth just last quarter alone, leading me to believe that the momentum in Europe is going to persist.

The company continues its infrastructure buildout in the region, which will help offset the current U.S. softness and will be the key revenue driver going forward. Since this region is not as exposed to tariffs as the U.S., I can see margins starting to pick back up and drive decent earnings growth going forward. I will be listening for comments surrounding the plans for further expansion in Europe and any other comments regarding the current position and whether they are satisfied with what they have right now. The company covers pretty much most of the major central European regions, so it will be interesting to see if they have more locations they would like to reach going forward.

Besides Europe, the company recently decided to buy New Classic Home Furnishings for $18m, which should have been closed already by now. It is a great addition after the successful acquisition of the Noble House portfolio. I believe the company will repeat what they did with the Noble House. That is, look at what items are underperforming and cut them out, and replace them with what should work, which usually means higher-margin products. It wasn’t a big acquisition, but the management expects to see decent results. So, I will be listening for the progress on the acquisition’s integration, like what kind of costs the company sees and what kind of long-term potential there is, specifically in terms of (hopefully) enhanced GMV and margin (hopefully) uplift.

Speaking of GMV, with all the positive growth in higher-margin products and demand, I will be looking for the GMV growth momentum to be sustained at around 20%, and ideally, accelerate. The current six facilities in Germany seem to be doing quite well, so I would like to see if this momentum can be sustained through higher utilization and cross-border efficiency, as the facilities have very good strategic locations to reach many places around Europe. Higher utilization means higher margins due to operational leverage that allows us to distribute fixed costs a lot more efficiently. All these initiatives support a margin recovery in the future, and I believe the management will succeed. We may continue to see some further downward pressure until then, but after all the integration and optimization of SKUs are finished, margins will start to trend upwards.

Is GCT A Buy Before Earnings?

I think it is worth taking a look at the company’s valuation again. I think I was being too conservative back in November when it came to my margin of safety. The company’s strong balance sheet and great profitability with a very low forward P/E ratio warrant us to be a bit more optimistic regarding its outlook. Below are the updated assumptions, which will mostly be very similar to the last one I did back in November, except that I am toning down my margin of safety requirements.

7% in sales for the next 5 years is probably still quite conservative, especially if the European operations maintain the momentum. For margins, I am assuming a gradual return to better profitability due to the management’s optimization initiatives while leaving the rest of the assumptions as they were historically. What I truly changed in my model is the discount factor I used for GCT. Instead of its elevated 13%, I went with a slightly lower 11%, which is still above what I typically use, which is 10%. That way, I am still being rather conservative, but I acknowledge the fact that the company’s operations have been going very well. With these updated assumptions, we get the following EPS projections. It is worth noting that they are still on the lower end of what analysts are projecting, and they have already been underestimating GCT’s potential.

With these EPS projections, we get the following fair value for the company.

It seems that the company is still trading at a decent discount to its fair value. One of the reasons I picked up more shares this morning. Another reason I picked up more shares is because I am looking to diversify some of my exposure to the AI growth story, which has done wonders, but it’s getting a little too heavy in my portfolio, so GCT was one of the better plays I see going forward that could be somewhat protected from the eventual downtrend in AI spending that hyperscalers are doing right now. If the company drops on the day of earnings, while operations remain solid, I will not hesitate to pick up more shares once again.

Risks To Consider

European expansion could hit some obstacles while the demand for the product begins to subside. This would essentially erase GCT’s growth story and margin recovery, and that would cause havoc on the company’s share price.

The margin erosion could last much longer than anticipated, decreasing the company’s profitability, which would lead to unattractive multiples valuation.

And in the broader sense, no company is safe from the eventual correction of the markets, no matter in which business segment it operates.
